rg_summary: In United States v. Cline, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 84665 (N.D.N.Y. Apr. 17, 2026), the pro se defendants filed objections to a magistrate judge’s report and recommendation in a civil forfeiture proceeding.  The court observed that the defendants’ arguments were “asserted with the misplaced confidence that has become a hallmark of an output of a [GenAI] tool,” and referred to “the [GenAI] tools that they used” in describing the defendants’ selective reading of the magistrate judge’s analysis. While the court did not impose sanctions or formally address the GenAI issue beyond these observations, it rejected all of the defendants’ arguments and adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation in its entirety.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Saunders v. Albertsons/Safeway, LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 84039 (D. Colo. Apr. 16, 2026), the defendant moved to strike the pro se plaintiff’s motion for costs on the grounds that she “relied on a fictional, [GenAI]-generated case.” The plaintiff acknowledged the error and apologized to the court and opposing counsel. The court declined to strike the motion, but warned the plaintiff that “citing to hallucinated cases may result in her filings being stricken without further analysis.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Strong v. Zubha POP Foods LLC, 2026 IL App (1st) 242451-U, 2026 Ill. App. Unpub. LEXIS 900 (Apr. 15, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs filed appellate briefs containing “copious citations to hallucinated authority” and references to “Centient AI,” some of which plaintiff “tried to obscure.” The defendant filed a motion for sanctions, to which the plaintiffs chose not to respond. The court initially struck the plaintiffs’ opening and reply briefs and gave the plaintiffs an opportunity to refile. Despite this second chance, the plaintiffs’ refiled briefs continued to rely on “unsupported arguments and illegitimate citations,” including “abundant citations to both hallucinated cases and fictitious holdings and quotations from existent cases.” The defendant filed a second motion for sanctions, which the plaintiffs again declined to address. Citing recent Illinois appellate authority holding that the “[f]lagrant and unprincipled use of [GenAI] without ensuring the accuracy of the generated response ‘is an abuse of the adversary system,’” the court found that the plaintiffs’ “persistent dependence on incoherent arguments, hallucinated authority, and fictitious case holdings has rendered review impossible.”  The court again struck the plaintiffs’ briefs, and then dismissed the appeal for failure to comply with Illinois Supreme Court Rule 341, which regulates both brief formatting and reliance on authorities.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Redmond v. Dairy Farmers of Am., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 82609 (D. Kan. Apr. 14, 2026), the attorney for the plaintiff cited a case that neither the defendant nor the court could locate, among other instances of quoting or citing cases that “do not contain such quotes or propositions.” The court “strongly suspect[ed] that these errors are attributable to the careless use of [GenAI]” and warned counsel that “further errors like these will lead to sanctions” under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the court’s recent standing order on the use of GenAI. The court granted the defendant’s motion on other grounds.

rg_summary: In Smith v. TD Auto Fin. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 81331 (N.D. Miss. Apr. 14, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s motion contained GenAI-hallucinated cases, which the non-signatory defendants brought to the court’s attention. The court noted that this was “not the first time” the plaintiff’s filings cited “fictitious legal authorities” and that the court had previously admonished the plaintiff against further fictitious citations. While acknowledging that the plaintiff had filed the motion before the court’s prior admonishment, Judge Maxwell reiterated his warning that further citations to “false legal authorities” would result in sanctions, “up to and including monetary penalties, defendants’ attorney fees, and dismissal of this action.” The court denied the motion for reconsideration on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hessert v. St. Dog Coal., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 80561 (D. Colo. Apr. 13, 2026), the court cautioned the pro se plaintiff to review the court’s “Standing Order Regarding the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (‘AI’) in Court Filings,” which requires all litigants to include an “AI Certification” with “‘every filing’ indicating whether [GenAI] was used in drafting the filing.” Judge Wang advised the plaintiff that his pro se status “does not exempt him from complying with the Standing Order” and warned that future non-compliant filings “may be stricken.”

rg_summary: In Diallo v. 50 E. Chestnut Condo. Ass’n, No. 23-cv-13985, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 77536 (N.D. Ill. Apr. 9, 2026), the pro se plaintiff cited a case in his response brief with an incorrect reporter citation that, upon investigation, did not support his asserted proposition. The court found that the citation “may have been fabricated, perhaps with the use of [GenAI] applications.” The court reminded all parties that Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 requires a reasonable inquiry into the accuracy of filings and that failure to review filings for accuracy may result in sanctions. The court granted in part and denied in part the defendants’ motions to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Michaelis v. Wilmington Sav. Fund Soc'y, 2026 N.Y. Misc. LEXIS 2581 (N.Y. Sup. Ct. Apr. 3, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed several repetitive motions in which “virtually every legal citation” was “‘hallucinated’ through the use of [GenAI].” The court opined that its rules require all litigants, including self-represented parties, to submit a certification confirming either that no GenAI was used in drafting, or that all GenAI-generated text, including citations, quotations, and legal analysis, was reviewed for accuracy. The court noted that, “[i]n the normal course,” the motion would have been denied without prejudice for lack of this certification and for clear evidence of unreviewed GenAI use but proceeded to address the merits due to “newly discovered” evidence. In doing so, the court denied the motions on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Ward v. Amazon.com Servs. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 73820 (W.D. Wash. Apr. 3, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to the defendant’s motion for arbitration contained incorrect citations to case law that was ultimately inapplicable to the facts at hand. The court informed the plaintiff that making misrepresentations of legal authority constitutes “making a false statement to a court” and could result in sanctions. While no sanctions were imposed, Judge Evanson warned the plaintiff that “any further fictitious legal authority” included in a future filing could result in an order to show cause.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Stanford v. Leinart, 2026 Tex. App. LEXIS 3109 (Tex. App. Apr. 2, 2026), the pro se appellant filed an appellate brief that contained at least one fabricated case citation and numerous misquotations of real cases on multiple occasions. The court identified the fabricated citation as an apparent “hallucinated” authority generated from the use of GenAI, citing recent Texas appellate authority holding that “citation of nonexistent cases that appear to have been generated by [GenAI] is unacceptable.” On multiple occasions, the appellant attributed quotations to real cases that appeared nowhere in the cited opinions, and cited cases for propositions they did not address. The court further admonished the appellant for fabricating a statutory requirement and “attempting to mislead this court” through a fictitious quote attributed to a statute. While the court found the appellant’s briefing “inadequate” and his “gross misrepresentations of the record and misstatements of the law” to be unacceptable, it nevertheless reached the merits of the appeal and affirmed the trial court’s order dismissing the appellant’s claims on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Chief Justice Bonnie Sudderth | Judge Dana Michelle Womack | Judge Brian Walker

rg_summary: In Hrdlichka v. Bengston, 2026 Ark. App. 205 (Ark. Ct. App. Apr. 1, 2026), the pro se appellant filed an appellate brief that contained multiple fictitious case citations not found in “any recognized legal database.” The court found that these fictitious citations, combined with pervasive failures and other procedural violations, constituted “fatal briefing deficiencies” that prevented meaningful appellate review, and dismissed the appeal. Noting that this was the court’s “first opportunity to consider the impact of fictitious cases being submitted to this court,” the court addressed at length the “growing and troubling practice” of submitting appellate briefs “generated in whole or in part through artificial-intelligence tools that contain fabricated, inaccurate, or nonexistent legal citations.” The court emphasized that the use of GenAI “does not relieve a litigant of the duty to verify the accuracy of citations,” that “technology, including [GenAI], is not a substitute for legal judgment, verification, and professional diligence,” and warned that filings containing fabricated citations “may be struck, and appropriate sanctions, such as dismissal, may be imposed where warranted.” The court concluded by urging litigants to be cognizant of the court’s awareness of issues related to pervasive GenAI use and their zero-tolerance policy for “fraud…in violation of [the court’s] rules.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties
judge: Judge Stephanie Potter Barrett | Chief Justice Mark Klappenbach | Judge Waymond M. Brown

rg_summary: In Heimkes v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condo. Owners Ass'n, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 70410 (S.D. Ala. Mar. 31, 2026), the attorney for plaintiffs filed multiple pleadings in a condominium dispute that contained fabricated citations and false statements of law, were discovered by both the court and defendants. The court issued two show cause orders detailing the misstatements and directing counsel to provide copies of the allegedly fabricated authorities or a sworn declaration explaining how they were generated, but counsel’s responses were untimely, failed to fully address the court’s concerns, and “raised additional issues.” Although counsel denied using GenAI, he attached hundreds of pages of output from CoCounsel (Westlaw’s a legal AI research service), that the court found “riddled with erroneous information” and described as “gibberish.” Counsel appeared at the first show cause hearing “woefully unprepared” to address the court’s concerns and then repeatedly sought continuances, citing health issues, before ultimately refusing to attend the rescheduled hearing despite the option to appear remotely. Finding that counsel’s pattern of conduct—including fabricated citations, misrepresentations, failure to correct errors after notice, and refusal to participate in the sanctions process—amounted to bad faith, the court sanctioned counsel under Rule 11, Alabama Rule of Professional Conduct 3.3 (the Alabama professional rule analogue to Rule 11), and its inherent authority. The court imposed a public reprimand requiring counsel to file the order in all active and future cases for twelve months, ordered counsel to pay $55,597.00 in attorney fees to defense counsel, referred the matter to the Alabama State Bar with a recommendation that counsel “be found incompetent to practice law,” and directed the order submitted for publication in the Federal Supplement.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In FTC v. Noland, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69162 (D. Ariz. Mar. 31, 2026), pro se individual defendants filed two motions which “appear[ed] to contain hallucinated or otherwise inaccurate case citations.”  The defendants acknowledged the existence of some errors but attributed them to having “proceeded pro se for over six years while subject to a comprehensive asset freeze, without access to Westlaw, Lexis, or comparable legal research resources.” The court found this excuse “unavailing,” citing recent authority holding that pro se litigants must follow the same rules of procedure as other litigants and that filing documents containing fictitious cases, whether generated by GenAI or “imagined” by the party, results in confusion and unnecessary work for opposing parties and the court. The court warned the defendants that they are responsible for the accuracy of any case citations in their filings and that the provision of future inaccurate citations may expose them to sanctions. The court denied the motions on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Montes v. Suns Legacy Partners LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 68888 (D. Ariz. Mar. 31, 2026), the defendant moved for Rule 11 sanctions against the attorneys for plaintiff after identifying at least eighteen instances of fictitious citations or fabricated quotations across four filings. The court found that the citations had “all the markings of a hallucinated case created by [GenAI] tools” and that counsel failed to conduct a “reasonable and competent inquiry” into the existence of the fabricated authorities. Counsel never took responsibility, instead attributing the errors to a law clerk, a LexisNexis cite-checking tool, “formatting” or “clerical” errors, and even to a “profound lack of diligence” by opposing counsel. The court rejected these explanations, noting that counsel’s Rule 11 obligations cannot be delegated and that running a brief through a cite-checking tool is no substitute for actually reading the cited cases. Finding that counsel’s conduct was not objectively reasonable, the court ordered both attorneys and their respective law firms, jointly and severally, to pay the defendant’s reasonable attorney fees incurred in discovering the fabricated authorities and briefing the sanctions motion. The court further ordered counsel to serve a copy of the order on their client, complete a CLE course on the ethical use of AI in law practice, and directed the Clerk to serve the order on the relevant state bars and on all judges in the district. The court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss and denied the motion to strike on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Ambrose v. Lee, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69545 (D. Conn. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se defendant filed a motion to dismiss in a defamation action that the court suspected was drafted, at least in part, using GenAI because of “serious misreading[s]” of cited authorities on two separate occasions. The defendant cited cases for propositions they did not support, and cases cited in support of a motion to strike that did not address relevant legal issues. The court announced a “no-tolerance policy for any briefing (AI-assisted or not) that hallucinates legal propositions or otherwise severely misstates the law,” warning that such filings “will often result in sanctions absent reasonable excuse.” The court emphasized that the failure to verify the accuracy of information generated by AI platforms implicates Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, which “applies fully to pro se litigants.”  The court granted in part and denied in part the motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Zavadovsky v. Republic of Austria, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69859 (D.D.C. Mar. 31, 2026), the defendant moved for sanctions following the plaintiffs’ inclusion of “nonexistent cases and fake quotations” in many of their filings. Though the plaintiffs “insist that their pleadings are not AI-generated,” the court flagged that one of the plaintiffs’ filings “read as if an unknown intelligent being were addressing Plaintiffs directly.” The court declined to impose sanctions but reminded the plaintiffs that submitting filings with “false, misleading, or nonexistent quotations or authority” violates Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and that any future filings with similar hallucinations could result in monetary and/or nonmonetary sanctions. Additionally, if the plaintiffs file new claims that appear “repetitive,” or “frivolous” or circumventive of “orders in other districts,” the court may file an order to show cause as to why “pre-filing restrictions” would be inappropriate.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Feldman v. Dist. 6 Bd. of Educ., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69063 (C.D. Ill. Mar. 31, 2026), the attorney for plaintiffs filed a response to the defendants’ motion to dismiss in a student disability and harassment case that cited nonexistent cases and attributed unsupported legal propositions to real cases on multiple occasions. The court observed in a footnote that certain cited cases “d[id] not appear to exist, along with many others,” and that other citations, “[a]lthough the case exists, the quote does not, and the proposition the case is cited for was not a point of discussion.” The court warned that “any future citation to nonexistent cases, cases for legal propositions not discussed or supported, or other false representations to the Court will result in a show cause hearing and consideration of sanctions.” The court granted in part and denied in part the defendants’ motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Ella & Ramin LLC v. Travelers Ins. Co., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69445 (N.D. Ill. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly misrepresented citations and caselaw in his briefs but openly admitted to using GenAI to prepare his filings. He also asserted that he took care to avoid legal misrepresentations. The court noted that “[f]ew of the cases [the plaintiff] cited support the proposition for which he cite[d] them,” that some were completely unrelated to the asserted propositions, and that one case could not be identified in any legal database despite the parallel citations provided. The plaintiff moved for Rule 11 sanctions against the defendants’ counsel for raising the possibility of GenAI-related errors in their reply brief, but the court denied the motion as frivolous, holding that the defendants’ counsel “plainly had a good-faith basis to call the Court’s attention to potential mistakes or misrepresentations of the law” and that attorneys are “in fact obligated to raise such mistakes when necessary.”  Citing recent Seventh Circuit authority, the court noted that “all litigants—represented and unrepresented—must read their filings and take reasonable care to avoid misrepresentations, factual and legal.” The court granted the defendants’ partial motion to dismiss in part and denied it in part on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Coleman v. Nexo Cap. Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 81931 (S.D. Ind. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs filed an opposition to the defendant’s motion for summary judgment which contained inaccurate citations, including a reference to a repealed statute and a nonexistent case. The court found it “unclear whether Plaintiffs’ citations are fabricated or erroneously cited,” and cautioned all parties to adhere to Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, “especially where [GenAI] may have been used to assist parties in preparing filings.” While the court did not impose sanctions, it warned that failure to comply with Rule 11 “authorizes sanctions, including the dismissal of the case.” The court granted the defendant’s motion for summary judgment on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Ford v. Sherwin-Williams, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69427 (D. Kan. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a motion for district judge review of a magistrate judge’s rulings that contained fictitious citations and quotations. The court observed that a citation in the plaintiff’s brief returned a case from a different court with different parties than represented, and suspected that the plaintiff used GenAI to produce the citation. Citing the court’s recent standing order (D. Kan. Standing Order 26-01) authorizing courts to “‘sua sponte strike any filing that appears to’ contain ‘fabricated or incorrect legal authority,’” Judge Crabtree cautioned the plaintiff that he will face sanctions—including “‘monetary sanctions’ or dismissal”—if he submits future filings containing “fabricated or incorrect legal authority.”  The court granted in part and denied in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Allen v. W. Governors Univ., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 69635 (D. Nev. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff repeatedly submitted filings containing citations to misrepresented and nonexistent legal authorities despite multiple warnings regarding his obligations under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court issued an order to show cause, and the plaintiff’s response itself contained additional fabricated quotations attributed to real cases that contained no such language. The defendants catalogued “over sixty misrepresented or fabricated citations” across the plaintiff’s filings. The court found that the plaintiff either fabricated authorities and misrepresented their origins, or “enlisted the assistance of [GenAI],” which generated the fabricated cases, and then “failed to check their validity (even after being specifically instructed to do so).” The court further found that the plaintiff knowingly misrepresented material facts in an attempt to overcome the statute of limitations, constituting bad faith. Weighing the Ninth Circuit’s dispositive sanction factors, the court concluded that lesser sanctions were unavailable given the plaintiff’s repeated violations and failure to provide a candid explanation and dismissed the case with prejudice as a sanction. The court also ordered monetary sanctions in the form of the defendants’ attorneys’ fees and costs incurred as a direct result of the plaintiff’s citations to nonexistent or misrepresented authorities.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Quandel Constr. Grp., Inc. v. Hunt Constr. Grp., Inc., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 70489 (S.D. Ohio Mar. 31, 2026), the attorneys for defendant filed an opposition memorandum that contained two incorrect case names, two incorrect case citations, and purported direct quotations from a case that did not contain the quoted language. The Plaintiff’s attorneys hypothesized that the errors were the product of a GenAI hallucination, but attorneys for the defendant denied using GenAI and sought leave to file a corrected brief, which the court granted. While the court noted that “[i]t does not appear that artificial intelligence was the culprit” based on counsel’s representations, it found that Hunt’s briefing had “failed to clarify how or why it provided the Court with two incorrect case names, two incorrect case citations, and improper direct quotations,” and emphasized that attorneys’ Rule 11 obligations to conduct a reasonable inquiry into the law apply “regardless of whether an attorney utilizes artificial intelligence in crafting legal contentions.” The court observed that false citations “damage judicial proceedings” whether or not GenAI was involved, and that no filing “‘should contain any citations—whether provided by [GenAI] or any other source—that’ a lawyer has not personally ‘read and verified.’” The court ordered all three of defendant’s attorneys to submit individual sworn submissions explaining their roles in preparing the brief and whether they used any AI tool in connection with the matter. The court denied the plaintiff’s motion on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Masterson v. Nyffeler, No. 25-2-11068-6 (Wa. Super. Mar. 31, 2026), the pro se plaintiff’s fourth iteration of the same lawsuit contained several issues, including using GenAI to generate numerous pleadings that flooded the docket. The plaintiff did not deny allegations of GenAI use. The court observed that while the use of AI “is not per se improper,” when a pro se litigant uses AI to generate “an inordinate number of pleadings, it is extremely burdensome and costly to the represented party,” and reminded all parties—including pro se litigants—of their obligation to comply with CR 11.  The court awarded attorney fees to the prevailing defendants and relieved them of any obligation to respond to further motions by the plaintiff unless ordered by the court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Dec v. Mullin, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 9085 (7th Cir. Mar. 30, 2026), the attorney for the plaintiff-appellant included two nonexistent case citations and a false quotation in her opening brief on appeal, which the court found had “tell-tale signs of [GenAI] hallucinations.” When questioned at oral argument, counsel could not explain the citations, denied using AI, and stated she “presumably copied and pasted that portion from another brief” but could not locate the source. In a post-argument letter, counsel reiterated that she did not use AI, took responsibility, and apologized.  Acknowledging the “promise and pitfalls” of AI in litigation, but expressing belief in the attorney’s “representation that she did not use AI,” the court reminded counsel that lawyers must not “knowingly misrepresent, mischaracterize, misquote, or miscite facts or authorities” and that violations “result in confusion and time wasted,” generating “unnecessary work on a case within a case.” The court publicly admonished counsel but declined to issue a show cause order for further sanctions, finding her acceptance of responsibility and apology persuasive and noting that the errors were few and “mainly supported the undisputed standard of review.” The court affirmed the dismissal on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Chief Judge Michael B. Brennan | Judge Frank H. Easterbrook | Judge Rebecca Laine Taibleson

rg_summary: In Williams v. Crystal Springs Apts. LLC, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67113 (D. Ariz. Mar. 30, 2026), the court addressed the pro se plaintiffs’ use of GenAI tools to draft their amended complaint in ruling on the plaintiffs’ application for leave to proceed in forma pauperis. Though the plaintiffs’ original complaint included a disclosure that certain AI tools were used for drafting purposes and confirmed that factual allegations were based on the plaintiffs’ “personal knowledge and documentation,” the amended complaint contained no such disclosure (despite appearing to have been drafted using AI as well), “[did] not establish any plausible basis for relief[,] and assert[ed] claims that are not recognized under existing law.” The court reminded the parties that GenAI tools are not prohibited but must be used with caution. As such, Judge Lanham ordered the plaintiffs to include a “separate declaration” attached to all future filings where “Generative AI” is used, in which the plaintiffs must (i) confirm that the filing was independently “reviewed and verified;” and (ii) identify the portion(s) that “incorporate[] [GenAI] outputs. The court warned that any party who submits a filing with “inaccurate or undeclared generative AI outputs” could face sanctions. The court ultimately dismissed the plaintiffs’ amended complaint “with limited leave to amend.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Souza v. City of Fitchburg, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67397 (D. Mass. Mar. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed an opposition to the defendants’ motion to dismiss that the court found was prepared, at least in part, using GenAI.  Judge Guzman observed that “[i]t is apparent to the Court that Plaintiff relied on [GenAI] in multiple instances to create his filings,” citing multiple instances in the plaintiff’s opposition where the GenAI program appeared to speak directly to the plaintiff, “using terms like ‘you’ and ‘your’ to refer to Plaintiff, and ‘he’ to refer to defense counsel.”  The court warned that GenAI “is unreliable for legal assistance and often ‘hallucinates,’” citing recent authority addressing GenAI hallucinations. The court granted the defendants’ motion to dismiss in full on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In 76 Route 6 Holdings Inc. v. Town of Yorktown, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 71964 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 30, 2026), the plaintiff’s attorney filed an opposition brief that attributed nonexistent quotations to real cases on multiple occasions. The court found that the purportedly quoted language “appears nowhere in the cited decision” and warned counsel that they “must ensure the accuracy of all quoted materials” pursuant to their duties under Rule 11, citing authority holding that attorneys must “confirm the existence and validity of [] the legal authorities on which they rely.” The court did not impose sanctions but cautioned that counsel “runs the risk of sanctions.” The court granted the defendants’ motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Kassaw v. Wal-Mart Corp., 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67874 (W.D.N.Y. Mar. 30, 2026), the defendant moved for Rule 11 sanctions against the pro se plaintiff, arguing that several of the  plaintiff’s filings contained hallucinated cases and misstatements of fact. The court declined to impose monetary sanctions, citing the plaintiff's pro se status and that the court had not previously admonished or warned him that sanctions could be imposed. However, the court directed the Clerk of Court to strike one of the plaintiff's memoranda from the docket because it “appears to include hallucinated cases.” The court further warned the plaintiff that “any future violation of Rule 11 may result in appropriate sanctions.” The court granted the defendant’s motion for summary judgment on all claims on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Fleck v. Del-One Fed. Credit Union, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 67368 (D. Or. Mar. 30, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss that contained misquoted local rules and a citation to a nonexistent case. The plaintiff had previously acknowledged using his company’s AI “systems” to “prepar[e] and format[]” his complaint and exhibits. The court confirmed that the purported citation “does not generate a case” and that the only source on Westlaw containing the purported quotation was the plaintiff’s own filing, noting that the plaintiff's “implicit invitation to make ‘findings’ that ‘mirror[]’ a hallucinated case” lent “further support” to the court’s assumption that the plaintiff used GenAI. Citing recent authority regarding GenAI hallucinations in litigation, the court reminded the plaintiff that Rule 11 requires all litigants to “read, and thereby confirm the existence and validity of, the legal authorities on which they rely,” and that relying on fabricated opinions “is an abuse of the adversary system.” The court warned that any further hallucinated legal citations will expose the plaintiff to sanctions. On other grounds, the court granted in part and denied in part the defendant's motion to dismiss.

rg_summary: In McCarthy, P.A. v. United States DEA, No. 24-2704, 2026 U.S. App. LEXIS 9000 (3d Cir. Mar. 27, 2026), the appellant’s counsel “relied on case overviews generated by AI and provided to him by a non-attorney . . . without verifying the existence or accuracy of the citations” for his opening brief. The Government noted the “factual and legal inaccuracies” in its response brief. The appellant’s counsel then failed to check the citations in his Reply Brief, characterizing erroneous citations as “a good faith effort to chronicle Agency disparities, [and that there were] some immaterial misstatements about the cited cases' tangential details.” In response to the court’s order to provide copies of the cases, the appellant’s counsel “disclosed, for the first time, that the seven summaries were inaccurate and that one authority was hallucinated by AI,” and admitted that he first suspected GenAI use months earlier. The court ordered counsel to show cause. After a hearing, the court found that the attorney violated his duty to provide competent representation to a client under Pa. R.P.C. 1.1. In considering sanctions, the court found that the attorney’s apologies and corrective measures were mitigating factors, along with the fact that the court had “not yet addressed the failure of attorneys to use AI with appropriate guardrails” and that it did not specifically ask the attorney to address the rule. The court imposed a sanction of reprimand, involving notifying “‘all other courts before whom [Attorney] is admitted to practice and the National Disciplinary Data Bank of this discipline’ and provide them with a copy of the opinion imposing this discipline.” The court noted that it would have imposed monetary sanctions had this not been the first opportunity to address GenAI use. In a dissenting opinion, Judge Roth wrote that this case called for more severe sanctions because of the multiple instances of sanctionable conduct, the fact that the attorney received early notice of his mistakes, and that the court should not have to “explicitly forewarn against improper AI use” when it falls within existing legal rules.

rg_summary: In Shaltry v. Benzing, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 66498 (E.D. Mich. Mar. 27, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed multiple motions which contained false quotations and citations that the court identified as apparent GenAI hallucinations. On multiple occasions, the plaintiff attributed quotations to real cases that appeared nowhere in those opinions and cited at least one case the court was unable to find at all. The court noted that the closest a cited case came to supporting the plaintiff’s proposition “actually cuts the other way.” Citing recent Sixth Circuit authority that states, “‘no brief, pleading, motion, or any other paper filed in any court should contain any citations—whether provided by [GenAI] or any other source—that a lawyer has not personally read and verified,’” the court emphasized that the plaintiff’s pro se status does not alleviate him of his duties under Rule 11. The court cautioned the plaintiff that “further misrepresentations of fact or law will result in a recommendation for sanctions,” and denied struck the plaintiff’s motions on other grounds.

rg_summary: In Amtrust N. Am. v. Liberty Mut. Ins. Co., No. A-2587-24, 2026 N.J. Super. Unpub. LEXIS 633 (Super. Ct. App. Div. Mar. 27, 2026), the plaintiff’s counsel “cite[d] to four cases in his merits brief that do not exist,” whch the court suspected was the result of GenAI misuse. When the defendants pointed out in their opposition brief that they could not find one of the cases, the plaintiff’s counsel merely responded by pointing to “the existence of a case . . . with a similar citation,” but otherwise defending its position. The court was “stunned by the carelessness and dismissiveness of this statement,” noting that citing hallucinated cases, as the plaintiff’s authorities seemed to be, violates both state rules of professional conduct and court rules. The court found that this conduct warranted a “rare” sanction in part because the plaintiff’s counsel “flippantly disregarded the error and failed to make the appropriate retraction” and “glibly ignored the issue in his reply brief, in contravention of his duty of candor to the tribunal.” The court imposed a $1,000 sanction on the plaintiff’s counsel and dismissed the complaint without prejudice.

rg_summary: In State Nat'l Ins. Co. v. Treadwell, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 72098 (N.D. Ala. Mar. 26, 2026), the attorney for defendants filed a response to a motion filed by plaintiff that contained two fabricated quotations attributed to two different cases, neither of which existed in the cited authorities or anywhere else. The court issued an order to show cause, and counsel admitted to using ChatGPT and OpenCase to draft the filing, confirming that the quotations were hallucinations of one or both GenAI tools and that he did not thoroughly verify the citations before filing. The court found counsel’s conduct “akin to contempt” under Rule 11, noting his “cavalier approach to these proceedings,” which included a late-filed and unedited response to the show cause order and tardiness to the show cause hearing. Citing its own prior decision emphasizing that citations to fake legal authority “pose a serious threat to the fair administration of justice,” the court imposed a public reprimand, ordering counsel to provide a copy of the order to opposing counsel and the presiding judge in every pending case in which he is counsel of record and directing the Clerk to submit the order for publication in the Federal Supplement. The court declined to refer the matter to the state bar, impose monetary sanctions, or suspend counsel, but warned that each of these options “is on the table” for any future infraction.

rg_summary: In Wallace v. Pennymac Loan Servs., LLC, No. 2:25-cv-02297-APG-EJY, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 64330 (D. Nev. rg_summary: In Baker v. Rastelli Foods LLC, No. 24-cv-08882-ESK-AMD, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 64242 (D.N.J. Mar. 26, 2026), the pro se plaintiffs included in their amended complaint cases that “are so inaccurately cited that the Court is unable to locate them, assuming they exist at all” and a “quotation that does not appear in the cited case.” In a separate case, the court had already warned the plaintiffs that failure to verify citations for similar errors may result in sanctions. The case was remanded for an order dismissing the complaint without prejudice on other grounds.

rg_summary: In Couvrette v. Wisnovsky, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 60329 (D. Or. Mar. 23, 2026), the court imposed sanctions on the plaintiffs and the plaintiffs’ attorney for filing three briefs containing fabricated cases and quotations, which the court held constituted violations of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and local rules. The plaintiffs continued to cite to nonexistent cases after the defendants called the errors to their attention and suggested GenAI misuse. In response to the defendants’ motion for sanctions, the plaintiffs simply deleted the non-existent citations and quotation marks while leaving the fabricated legal contentions unchanged. Plaintiffs argued that “[t]he inclusion of inaccurate citations was inadvertent and the result of reliance on an automated legal citation tool” and that they had taken proper corrective action. Defendants then re-filed their motion for sanctions. At the sanctions hearing, the court found that the plaintiffs failed to take corrective action and instead “attempted a coverup,” violating Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the Oregon Rules of Professional Conduct. The court also found the plaintiff himself was likely the person using GenAI to draft and “share[d] responsibility [with his counsel] for the use of non-existent cases and fabricated quotations,” thus warranting sanctions against the plaintiff as well. As sanctions, the court struck without leave to refile plaintiffs’ summary judgment briefing; vacated the order allowing former local counsel to withdraw and ordered him show cause why he should not be subject to sanctions; assessed plaintiffs’ counsel a $15,000 fine to be paid to the clerk of court; requested defendants submit a bill of costs and reasonable attorney fees associated with their response to the fraudulent citations; and dismissed the plaintiffs’ claims with prejudice. In a later order, the court accepted defendant’s attorneys’ fees as reasonable, and ordered plaintiffs’ counsel and local counsel to pay defendants attorney fees totaling $94,704.38. Local counsel was ordered to pay $14,205.66, and plaintiffs’ counsel was ordered to pay the remaining $80,498.72.

rg_summary: In Gentry v. Thompson, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 58718 (E.D. La. Mar. 20, 2026), defense counsel for the City of New Orleans filed a memorandum in support of a motion to dismiss that contained nine hallucinated case citations that the court observed were “likely” generated by GenAI. After the court issued an order to show cause, the primary drafting attorney appeared and admitted to using ChatGPT to find supporting caselaw and conceded that he neither checked the citations nor read the cited cases before filing. His supervising attorney acknowledged that he reviewed and revised other sections of the memorandum but failed to verify the hallucinated citations. The court found three attorneys whose names were on the filing violated Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11 by failing to conduct a reasonable inquiry into the legal authority cited in the brief. The court sanctioned the drafting attorney $250 and his supervising attorney $1,000, reasoning that the supervisor bore greater responsibility given his roughly thirty years of experience and his duty to ensure the accuracy of filings by attorneys under his supervision. The court declined to sanction the Chief Deputy City Attorney but issued a formal admonishment, and the newly appointed City Attorney outlined remedial steps the office was undertaking, including a department-wide meeting and development of a written GenAI policy.

rg_summary: In Blair v. Sanctuary Bluff Homeowners Ass'n, 2026 Ky. App. Unpub. LEXIS 180 (Ky. Ct. App. Mar. 20, 2026), the opposing party moved to strike the pro se plaintiff’s initial appellant brief and dismiss the appeal due to nine hallucinated citations and repeatedly misquoting precedent. The plaintiff admitted to using GenAI and catching the mistakes, but claimed to have filed the wrong draft. The court denied the motion to dismiss but granted the motion to strike the brief and allowed the plaintiff to refile with a certification “that her brief was prepared without AI.” Ten days later, the plaintiff submitted a new brief certifying that while she used GenAI for editing purposes, she did not use it for drafting or research. The opposing party renewed its motion to dismiss, as the refiled brief again contained hallucinated precedent—an allegation to which the plaintiff admitted. The court again denied the motion to dismiss “in an abundance of grace.” However, when the plaintiff then submitted a reply brief with yet another hallucinated case and without the required GenAI certification, the court ruled that this “deliberate disregard for a Court’s ruling . . . likely sabotaged her own litigation.” The court nevertheless addressed the case on its merits and dismissed the appellant’s appeal, evidently in part because of her pattern of hallucinated citations.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Prososki v. Regan, 2026 Neb. LEXIS 38, 321 Neb. 38 (Neb. Mar. 20, 2026), counsel for the appellant in a dissolution of marriage appeal filed a brief containing numerous fictitious case citations, real cases with fabricated quotations and mischaracterized holdings, and fictitious quotations. The appellee moved to strike the brief and dismiss the appeal, asserting that the errors were the product of GenAI. Counsel denied using GenAI, attributing the errors to “sloppy” copying and pasting from Westlaw and having filed the wrong draft due to “a screen crack on his laptop,” but the court found this explanation “lack[ed] credibility” given the volume of fictitious quotations that could not be located anywhere in Nebraska jurisprudence. Noting that this presented a novel issue for Nebraska courts, the court held that the submission of fictitious authority, whether through GenAI or not, “can be resolved by application of [Nebraska’s] existing rules of professional conduct and court rules,” including the duties of candor toward the tribunal, competence, diligence, and good faith argumentation. The court struck the appellant's brief, dismissed his appeal, and referred his counsel to the Counsel for Discipline of the Nebraska Supreme Court.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In State v. Coleman, 2026-Ohio-965, 2026 Ohio App. LEXIS 988 (Ohio Ct. App. Mar. 20, 2026), defense counsel in a criminal appeal alleged prosecutorial misconduct by filing an application to reopen, in part based on allegedly “inflammatory, improper statements” by the prosecutor. That application contained fabricated transcript quotations, which defense counsel admitted was generated by ChatGPT, allegedly as a result of his paralegal using the tool without adequate supervision or verification. However, the purported quotes by the prosecutor were never made, which the court held impugned not only the prosecutor but also the trial judge (implying that the court permitted such improper statements). After the State identified the fabrications in its responsive filing and moved for sanctions, counsel neither withdrew nor amended the application, and subsequently appealed the denial to the Ohio Supreme Court without disclosing the fabricated content. Two months after the sanctions hearing, counsel filed a motion in an unrelated case that contained an embedded ChatGPT prompt in the text of the filing itself. And although counsel eventually proposed “remedial measures,” including the creation of an internal AI policy for his firm, that policy itself “bore the hallmarks” of having itself been generated by GenAI. Finding bad faith based on counsel’s familiarity with GenAI tools yet failing to verify citations, the significant nature of the hallucinations, and his failure to take corrective action, the court imposed comprehensive sanctions: a $2,000 monetary fine, referral to the Office of Disciplinary Counsel, a two-year requirement to serve the sanctions order on the presiding judge of every court in which counsel enters a new case, a two-year certification and verification requirement for all filings, six hours of mandatory CLE on AI ethics, striking of the fabricated application, removal as counsel, and written apologies to the individuals defamed by the fabricated filing.

rg_summary: In Sheerer v. Panas, 2026 Cal. App. LEXIS 170 (Cal. Ct. App. Mar. 19, 2026), the pro se respondent submitted a respondent’s brief that which contained fabricated citations to nonexistent cases, fabricated quotations attributed to real cases, and authorities cited for propositions they did not address. In a declaration, the respondent attributed the errors to his use of GenAI and his failure to verify citations, averring that he “did not knowingly submit false information to the Court.” The court emphasized that “no brief, pleading, motion, or any other paper filed in any court should contain any citations—whether provided by [GenAI] or any other source—that the attorney responsible for submitting the pleading has not personally read and verified.” The court also noted that its redress for false and missing citations is “not limited to disregarding a brief” and includes monetary sanctions under Cal. Rules of Court, Rule 8.276(a)(4). However, the court declined to impose monetary sanctions given the respondent’s admission of error amongst other factors. The court reversed the order on other grounds.

rg_summary: In Brownfield v. Cherokee Cnty. Sch. Dist. No. 35, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 57452 (E.D. Okla. Mar. 19, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a motion for sanctions that contained eight citations identified by the defendant as either fictitious or inaccurate. The court issued a sua sponte order to show cause, and the plaintiff acknowledged using GenAI to assist in organizing legal research for the motion without verifying the citations for accuracy. The court found that the plaintiff’s submission of fictitious and inaccurate citations violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b), emphasizing that pro se litigants “must ‘follow the same rules of procedure that govern other litigants.’” In an attempt to mitigate sanctions, the plaintiff voluntarily completed a two-month AI program covering hallucinations and ethical considerations and pledged not to use GenAI in any future matter before the court prior to the show cause hearing. The court ordered the plaintiff to pay the defendant $500 for attorney’s fees incurred researching the fabricated cases, finding this the minimum sanction sufficient to deter and punish considering mitigating factors, and warned that future Rule 11 violations would result in harsher penalties.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In In re Est. of Carey v. Whitman, 2026 Wash. App. LEXIS 562 (Wash. Ct. App. Mar. 19, 2026), the pro se appellant’s briefs contained citations to “incorrect, chimerical, or inapplicable case law” throughout, including citations to cases that did not exist and misquotations or misrepresentations of the holdings of cases that did exist. The court declined to review arguments premised on fictitious authority, holding that citations to fictitious legal authority “equates to a lack of legal authority” under Rule of Appellate Procedure 10.3(a)(6), which requires parties to provide citations to relevant legal authority in support of their arguments. The court also found the appeal “so devoid of merit that there was no possibility for reversal,” thus rendering it frivolous. While the court declined to impose independent sanctions for the appellant's use of fictitious case law and failure to comply with the Rules of Appellate Procedure, it did impose sanctions for the appellant’s frivolous appeal and granted attorneys fees to appellees

rg_summary: In Brooks v. Amazon Data Servs., Inc., No. 2:25-cv-01294-HL (D. Or. Mar. 18, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a response to the defendant’s motion for summary judgment that contained citations and authorities that “appear to be inaccurate or non-existent.” The court stated that it had “reason to believe” the plaintiff used a GenAI tool in the preparation of the brief and that the tool “hallucinated” certain legal authorities. Although the court recognized that pro se litigants “may seek technological assistance when preparing filings,” it emphasized that it is the litigant’s responsibility to ensure that all filings “are accurate, truthful, and grounded in existing law and authority” under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11(b). The court prohibited the plaintiff from using any GenAI tool to draft or assist in drafting any future filings in the case and ordered that all future briefs or motions include a certification under penalty of perjury that the filing “was prepared solely by [the plaintiff] and was not drafted or assisted by any [GenAI] tool.” The court warned that failure to comply may result in sanctions, including dismissal of claims or striking of filings.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Killebrew v. McKiddy, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 56792 (E.D. Mich. Mar. 18, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed multiple improper filings, including a frivolous motion to dismiss his own case and an unauthorized supplemental filing, both of which the court struck. During the scheduling conference, the court warned the plaintiff that if he uses GenAI to draft his filings, he “must review all legal citations to ensure they are accurate,” and that “[f]actitious citations will not be tolerated and will result in sanctions.” The court also warned the plaintiff that he will face sanctions if he continues to “clog[] the docket with more improper filings.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hoots v. Walker, 2026 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 57586 (E.D. Tex. Mar. 18, 2026), the pro se plaintiff filed a complaint and sought to proceed in forma pauperis against the defendant. After Magistrate Judge Payne issued a report and recommendation recommending dismissal under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2), the plaintiff filed objections. In its order overruling the plaintiff’s objections and dismissing the case with prejudice, the court reminded the parties that under Local Rule CV-11(g), “[a]ll litigants remain responsible for the accuracy and quality of legal documents produced with the assistance of [GenAI] technology” and that any litigant who “chooses to employ [GenAI] technology” remains “bound by the requirements of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and must review and verify all content to ensure that it complies with all such standards.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Francois v. Vive Fin., LLC, 2026 Fla. App. LEXIS 2084 (Fla. 4th Dist. Ct. App. Mar. 18, 2026), the pro se appellant filed briefs in both the trial court and on appeal that cited multiple authorities that “do not exist.” Citing its own recent precedent cautioning pro se litigants about the submission of fictitious case law, the court emphasized that “[a]ttorneys and pro se litigants ‘are responsible for the content of their submissions to the court’” and that “‘[t]hose who use [GenAI] to assist in these submissions must ensure their accuracy.’” The court further observed that GenAI “may assist in research and drafting, but it cannot replace the lawyer’s or litigant’s duty to ensure that every authority cited is real, accurate, and applicable.” The court declined to impose sanctions but cautioned the appellant that future filings containing nonexistent authorities may result in sanctions. The court affirmed the trial court on other grounds.
